text: The surgeon works on the posterior side of the gallbladder, clearing the area around the visible artery. The cystic duct is observed diving deeper into the tissue, prompting further examination. A large posterior artery branch is identified and maneuvered out of the way.

The surgeon toggles between views, attempting to dissect the infundibulum of the gallbladder, which appears fused to the cystic duct at the exit point. The green indesigning does not illuminate the cystic duct.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, revealing the inferior margin of the right hepatic lobe.

The surgeon observes the bifurcation of a posterior artery branch near the cystic plate, which appears highly vascular. Efforts are made to preserve the branching vessels visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon observes extensive pericholecystic fluid along the cystic plate, spanning almost the entire length of the gallbladder. The fluid appears to be very intrahepatic, which may be visually deceiving.
The surgeon observes the complete collapse of the cystic plate, nearly entering the gallbladder. The liver appears fatty, while the cystic plate is clearly visible.
